label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example because it is a primary Randomized Clinical Trial (RCT) that uses a smartphone app and a Reinforcement Learning (AI) algorithm to deliver a real-world lifestyle intervention. CONTENT: An AI-Powered Lifestyle Intervention vs Human Coaching in the Diabetes Prevention Program. The study compares an AI-led intervention (via mobile app and digital scale) to human coaching. The AI used a reinforcement learning algorithm to personalize messaging, successfully achieving weight loss and physical activity goals in adults with prediabetes.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example because it proposes a "Just-In-Time Adaptive Intervention" (JITAI) using a digital assistant on a smartphone. It utilizes Machine Learning (contextual bandits) and NLP to interact with users in the real world. CONTENT: Increasing physical activity using a just-in-time adaptive digital assistant supported by machine learning. The paper describes a system with three components: educational conversations via NLP, a nudge engine using reinforcement learning for hyper-personalized cues based on real-time data (GPS, weather), and a generative AI Q&A feature.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example describing a protocol for an RCT of an app-based digital assistant ("MoveMentor") that employs Large Language Models (LLMs) and Reinforcement Learning for personalized coaching. CONTENT: MoveMentor—examining the effectiveness of a machine learning and app-based digital assistant to increase physical activity. The intervention uses an app to provide educational conversations, just-in-time nudges, and chat-based Q&A, adapting in real-time to user data to increase physical activity.

label: POS
text: REASONING: This is a positive example because it details the design of a smartphone application that uses empirical data mining to create a self-learning module for timing physical activity reminders. CONTENT: The Design and Development of a Personalized Leisure Time Physical Activity Application. The study develops the "PAUL" app based on behavior change theories and data mining of historical running data. It features a self-learning module to optimize the timing of reminders and feedback, demonstrating a direct application of AI principles in a user-facing mobile tool.

label: NEG
text: REASONING: This is a negative example because it is a Scoping Review of existing methodologies, not a primary empirical study testing a specific intervention. CONTENT: A scoping review of methodologies for applying artificial intelligence to physical activity interventions. The review aggregates 24 studies on ML, DL, and RL in physical activity but does not present a new experimental intervention or primary data collection.

label: NEG
text: REASONING: This is a negative example because it utilizes Agent-Based Modeling (ABM) for simulation rather than a direct smartphone-based intervention with real-world users. CONTENT: Leveraging agent-based modeling and a randomized intervention to advance childhood physical activity. The protocol describes creating an ABM to simulate complex systems and effect pathways in childhood physical activity, using the model to extrapolate strategies rather than testing an AI app directly on users.

label: NEG
text: REASONING: This is a negative example because it focuses on developing a conceptual model to inform simulations (Agent-Based Modeling), lacking a direct AI-driven app component for real-time user intervention. CONTENT: Developing a Conceptual Model for Collective Patterns and Leisure-Time Physical Activity Inequalities. The paper aims to capture dynamic interactions to conceptualize and inform an agent-based model for simulating physical activity inequalities, which is a theoretical/modeling task rather than an mHealth application.

label: NEG
text: REASONING: This is a negative example because it focuses on validating behavioral models for simulation using automated knowledge extraction, falling under the excluded category of simulation/ABM studies. CONTENT: Validating and constructing behavioral models for simulation and projection using automated knowledge extraction. The paper proposes a method to extract evidence into a knowledge graph to construct or validate Agent-Based Models (ABM), focusing on simulation methodology rather than real-world physical activity promotion via smartphones.
